title: Former Science Advisor John Holdren Comments on Trump's Executive Order Reviving President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology
summary: Professor John P. Holdren welcomes the belated creation of a Trump administration President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST) to work with Office of Science and Technology Policy Director Kelvin Droegemeier on some of the important issues of national policy on science, technology, and innovation. Historically, PCAST and its predecessor group, the President's Science Advisory Committee, have played important roles connecting the White House with the views of top scientists, engineers, and innovators from business, universities, and civil-society organizations.
body: I welcome the belated creation of a Trump administration President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology ( PCAST) to work with Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) Director Kelvin Droegemeier on some of the important issues of national policy on science, technology, and innovation. Historically, PCAST and its predecessor group, the President's Science Advisory Committee, have played important roles connecting the White House with the views of top scientists, engineers, and innovators from business, universities, and civil-society organizations. The previous administration's PCAST was announced, fully formed, at a meeting with President Obama on April 27, 2009, barely three months past his first inauguration. It got off to a flying start and, over the course of Obama's two terms, completed nearly forty studies requested by the President, many of them leading to major new initiatives applying science and technology to national goals. The new PCAST has three big handicaps by comparison: it is starting more than two and a half years later in the game; it is working under a president whose apparent interest in inputs from scientists is minimal; and the crucial issues at intersection of science and technology with the immense challenge of climate change do not seem to be on its stated agenda at all. Even so, OSTP Director Droegemeier is a strong leader, and the initial tranche of seven appointees includes many distinguished individuals (albeit with minimal representation from academia and none from civil society). One may hope that the remaining appointments will bring more sectoral diversity and that, despite the late start, the group will be able to make some significant contributions. The latest from Belfer See more Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. Recommended citation Holdren, John P. "Former Science Advisor John Holdren Comments on Trump's Executive Order Reviving President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology." Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School, October 23, 2019. _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: John Holdren welcomes the Trump administration's revival of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST) to collaborate with OSTP Director Kelvin Droegemeier on national science and innovation policy. He notes the historical importance of such advisory bodies in connecting the White House with top experts from various sectors. However, he contrasts the new PCAST unfavorably with the Obama-era council, which was formed quickly and produced numerous impactful studies. Holdren identifies three major handicaps for the Trump PCAST: its late start over two and a half years into the term, a president with minimal apparent interest in scientific input, and the apparent omission of climate change from its agenda. Despite these issues, he acknowledges Droegemeier's strong leadership and the distinction of the initial appointees, while hoping for greater sectoral diversity in future appointments and some significant contributions from the group.
result.main_content_zh: 约翰·霍尔德伦对特朗普政府恢复总统科技顾问委员会（PCAST）表示欢迎，该委员会将与科技政策办公室主任凯尔文·德罗格迈尔合作，处理国家科技与创新政策的重要议题。他指出此类顾问机构在连接白宫与来自商界、大学和民间社会的顶尖科学家、工程师和创新者观点方面具有历史重要性。然而，他将新的PCAST与奥巴马时期的委员会进行了不利对比，后者迅速成立并完成了近四十项总统委托的研究，其中许多促成了应用科技实现国家目标的重大新举措。霍尔德伦指出特朗普的PCAST存在三大缺陷：起步过晚（在任期两年半后才成立）、总统对科学建议的兴趣明显不足，以及关键的气候变化相关科技议题似乎完全未列入其既定议程。尽管如此，他承认德罗格迈尔是强有力的领导者，且首批七名任命者中包含许多杰出人士（尽管学术界代表极少，民间社会代表缺失）。他希望后续任命能带来更多的部门多样性，并且尽管起步较晚，该小组仍能做出一些重要贡献。
